speaker: doctor
text: Good morning. Let's start with your vitals — your temperature today is 37.9 °C, which is slightly elevated but still within a low-grade fever range.

speaker: doctor
text: Your systolic blood pressure is 152 mmHg — that’s above the target of 140 for someone with diabetes. We’ll want to monitor this closely.

speaker: doctor
text: Based on your lab work, HbA1c, and clinical presentation, I’m confirming the diagnosis of Type 2 Diabetes Mellitus.

speaker: patient
text: Yes, I’ve been trying to watch my diet and walk daily. But lately I’ve felt more tired than usual, and my feet feel a bit tingly.

speaker: doctor
text: Your heart rate is 64 bpm — well within normal limits. That’s reassuring, especially given your recent symptoms.

speaker: patient
text: I’ve also had some mild dizziness when standing up quickly — not every day, but maybe two or three times this week.

speaker: doctor
text: That’s important to note. Dizziness on standing could suggest orthostatic changes — we’ll check your BP in both positions next.

speaker: doctor
text: You’re currently on Atorvastatin 40mg at bedtime for cholesterol management. How has that been tolerated? Any muscle aches or fatigue?

speaker: patient
text: Actually, yes — my legs have felt heavy and sore sometimes, especially after walking. And I’ve been sleeping more than usual.

speaker: doctor
text: Your diastolic pressure today is 70 mmHg — not concerning on its own, but let’s keep it in context with your symptoms and other readings.

speaker: patient
text: I did check my blood sugar at home yesterday — it was 182 mg/dL two hours after dinner. Not sure if that’s high enough to worry about.

speaker: doctor
text: Given your muscle symptoms and borderline fatigue, I’m going to reduce your Atorvastatin dose to 20mg at night. We’ll reassess in four weeks.

speaker: doctor
text: Just rechecked your pulse — it’s now 50 bpm. That’s a notable drop from the 64 we saw earlier. Have you noticed any lightheadedness or pauses in your heartbeat?

speaker: patient
text: Now that you mention it — yes, last night I felt like my heart skipped a beat a couple of times, and I woke up short of breath once.

speaker: patient
text: That reading from earlier — the 37.9 °C — matches what I measured at home this morning too. No chills, but I’ve had a dry cough for about three days.

speaker: doctor
text: Let’s get an ECG and check your electrolytes — the bradycardia plus cough and low-grade fever raise concern for possible myocarditis or viral involvement.

speaker: patient
text: I’ve also noticed my ankles are a little swollen since yesterday — not severe, but definitely new.

speaker: doctor
text: We’ll add a basic metabolic panel and troponin. In the meantime, hold the metformin until we rule out acute illness — it’s safer during febrile states.

speaker: doctor
text: Just took your seated diastolic again — it’s dropped to 60 mmHg. Combined with the bradycardia and swelling, this suggests possible early decompensation. We’ll need urgent follow-up tomorrow.

speaker: patient
text: Okay — should I go to the ER tonight, or is tomorrow morning soon enough?
